1:
Development of a decision support
system for assessing the supply
chain risk mitigation strategies:
an application in Indian petroleum
supply chain
Pradeep Kumar Tarei
Vinod Gupta School of Management, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur,
Kharagpur, India
Jitesh J. Thakkar
Department of Industrial and Systems Engineering,
Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, Kharagpur, India, and
Barnali Nag
Vinod Gupta School of Management, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur,
Kharagpur, India
Abstract
Purpose ‚Äì The purpose of this paper is to develop a decision support system (DSS) to assist supply chain (SC)
risk managers to select a suitable risk management (RM) strategy and expedite the implementation of
corresponding RM enablers. The relationship between RM strategies and RM enablers is explored by
identifying the underlying factors between them, which is further used to build the DSS.
Design/methodology/approach ‚Äì The DSS is built by integrating heterogeneous techniques. A systematic
review approach is employed to explore both proactive and reactive RM enablers, and they are further mapped
to various RM strategies by using correspondence analysis (CA). An in-depth interview is conducted to develop
the rules for constructing the decision system. A rule-based fuzzy inference system (FIS) is utilized to counteract
the uncertainty involved in the decision variables. The efficacy of the proposed DSS is demonstrated by
considering two conjectural scenarios in the case of Indian petroleum SC (IPSC).
Findings ‚Äì The results reveal three primary underlying factors between the risk mitigation strategies viz. SC
managers‚Äô preparedness to face risk, organization‚Äôs resource capability to deal with risk and the sophistication of
the implementation of the RM enablers; with explained variances of 37%, 29% and 22%, respectively. Risk
avoidance strategy comprises of RM enablers such as supplier evaluation, technology adaption, information
security, etc. Whereas, the risk-sharing strategy includes revenue sharing, insurance, collaboration, public-
private-partnership, etc. as essential RM enablers. The DSS recommends risk-mitigation and risk-sharing as
effective RM strategies for the IPSC under the considered scenarios.
Research limitations/implications ‚Äì This paper develops a decision support framework for recommending
an effective risk mitigation strategy and outranking the corresponding enablers. The study explicitly focuses on
the risk mitigation step of the supply chain risk management (SCRM) process. Pre- and post-risk mitigation steps
of the SCRM process, such as risk assessment and risk monitoring are beyond the scope of this research.
Originality/value ‚Äì The operational procedure of the proposed DSS is explained by considering a real-life
case of petroleum SC in the Indian scenario. The unique contributions of this study are presented as theoretical
implications and managerial propositions in the context of a developing country.
Keywords Supply chain risk management, Risk mitigation, Decision support system
Paper type Research paper
JMTM
32,2
506

to enhance the quality of this paper.

1. Introduction and background:
The ever-growing global competition and enormously uncertain business environment are
causing a modern supply chain (SC) to become vulnerable and hence more susceptible to risk.
The continuous attempt to achieve profitability and the emergence of recent manufacturing
practices (like globalization, single sourcing, lean manufacturing, etc.) are pushing modern SCs
toward risk (Tarei et al., 2018). The risk in SC leads to serious repercussions like inferior
product quality, devastation of property and machinery, loss of firm‚Äôs reputation, delivery
delays (Cousins et al., 2004), manufacturing disruption (Zhang et al., 2018), conflict among
various shareholders (Craighead et al., 2007) and sharp decline in firm‚Äôs share price
(Hendricks and Singhal, 2005). A practical implementation of supply chain risk management
(SCRM) improves not only the financial performance of the firm (Fan et al., 2017) but also the
manufacturing effectiveness and overall performance of SC (Thun and Hoenig, 2011). The
process of SCRM starts with the identification of risk drivers, assessment of the critical
drivers and implementation of mitigation practices. In this regard, most of the research has
been conducted toward identification and assessment of the potential risk drivers as
compared to the risk mitigation phase (Ghadge et al., 2013). Hence, we explicitly focus on the
‚ÄúRisk mitigation‚Äù stage of the SCRM cycle and discuss the research limitations about the risk
mitigation phase in the following section.
Risk mitigation is one of the crucial phases of the SCRM cycle, which involves the selection
of an effective risk management (RM) strategy and implementation corresponding RM
enablers to nullify the adverse impact of risk. In most of the existing literature, RM enablers
(also referred to as risk responses, risk protection strategies, mitigation tactics, redesign
principles, etc) and RM strategies are used interchangeably (Vlajic et al., 2012). We, however,
argue that RM enablers are actual SC practices that are implemented at the operational level
in the real-world business environment to reduce the overall riskiness of the SC (definitional
significance of RM strategy is highlighted in next paragraph). We prefer to choose the term as
RM enablers (abbreviated as RMEs) and use it consistently hereafter. These RMEs are
primarily categorized into two groups, viz. proactive (preventive) RMEs and reactive
(recovery) RMEs. Proactive RMEs show their impact beforehand and are primarily
responsible for reducing the probability of occurrence of a risk event (Sheffi and Rice, 2005;
Meng, 2019). On the contrary, reactive RMEs are rather effect-oriented, which can only show
an impact after the occurrence of the risk event and are responsible for suppressing the
adverse effects of an unforeseen event (Thun and Hoenig, 2011). The consideration of both
proactive and reactive RMEs in a single model in the domain of SCRM is relatively scanty
(Ho et al., 2015; Rajagopal et al., 2017).
Roh et al. (2014) defined SC strategy as a plan, which is decided by the top executives
(following the corporate strategy) of a firm which precedes SC practices. The definition of RM
strategies (abbreviated as RMSs) used in this research is adapted from the above source.
Hence, we conceive and define RMS as a plan (not action), which is decided by the top
executives (aligned with the corporate strategy) of a firm to counteract the future (adverse)
impact of the risk. Though numerous attempts have been devoted to evaluate various RM
strategies under different operational scenario (Aqlan and Lam, 2015; Qazi et al., 2018), the
existing literature has failed not only to highlight the distinction between RMEs and RMSs
but also to provide an established relationship between RMEs and RMSs for the treatment
of risk.
In the era of industry 4.0, the integration of modern (intelligent) tools and real-time
information processing systems help SC to achieve organizational competency. This
brings a new opportunity for managers to react to and manage SC risk in a shorter time
frame (G‚Ç¨uller et al., 2015). The embedded intelligence helps SCM to move from supporting
decisions to assigning them and, ultimately, to predict which decisions need to be
made (Butner, 2010). Some recent studies (Schl‚Ç¨uter and Sprenger, 2016; Kirazli and
DSS to assist
SC risk
managers in

3:
Moetz, 2015) on digitalization in SCRM have focused on identification of potential
risks through implementation of technologies. Saridakis et al. (2018) highlighted the
assessment of RMSs and the implementation of corresponding RMEs to nullify the
devastation effect of risk. In the context of SCRM, limited studies have been performed,
which primarily focus on the development of a decision support system (DSS) to assist SC
risk managers.
Responding to all the research limitations mentioned above, we frame the following
research questions (RQ) for the current study.
RQ1. What is the importance of various proactive and reactive RMEs, and how can they
be considered simultaneously in an SCRM model?
RQ2. What is the significance of different RMSs, and how can they be linked with the
corresponding RMEs?
RQ3. How can a DSS be developed which can assist the SC managers in making decisions
about the selection of an RMS and hence the implementation of RMEs?
The remainder of this paper is structured as follows: Literature related to the risk mitigation
phase of the SCRM process and the techniques adapted to build the DSS are reviewed in
Section 2. Section 3 deals with the conceptualization and development of the DSS. A
demonstration of the proposed DSS in the context of a case industry is provided in Section 4.
The unique contributions of the study are discussed in Section 5. Finally, the research paper
ends with conclusions and future research directions in Section 6.
2. Literature review and methodologies adopted:
In this section, we provide a brief overview of relevant literature and (review of) various
methodologies that are adopted from multiple disciplines to build the DSS.
2.1 Supply chain risk management process
Baryannis et al. (2018b) generalized the definition of SCRM (after surveying prior definitions):
‚Äúas a process that encompasses the collaborative and coordinated efforts of all parties involved
in a SC to reduce vulnerability and increase robustness and resilience of the supply chain,
ensuring profitability and continuity.‚Äù The various stages of SCRM process consist of
identification, assessment, mitigation and monitoring of risks (Baryannis et al., 2018b). Risk
classification and identification have been exhaustively discussed in the SCRM literature
(Christopher and Peck, 2004; Wagner and Bode, 2008; Ghadge et al., 2013; Tarei et al., 2018).
There is relatively less research that examines the effectiveness of alternative SCRM
strategies in the different contextual environment (Chang et al., 2015; Ayag et al., 2013). Hence,
the current research primarily focuses on the risk mitigation step of the SCRM process and
summarizes the relevant articles in Tables 1.
There are a few limitations to the studies above. First, very few modeling efforts have been
attempted considering both proactive and reactive RMEs in a single framework. Second,
distinct studies have been done to assess the RMEs and RMSs, but to the best of our
knowledge no research has been conducted to link the relationship between RMEs and
corresponding RMSs. As shown in Tables 1, the past decade has experienced accelerated
growth of literature by heterogeneous methodologies (conceptual, qualitative, quantitative
and interdisciplinary) in the domain of SCRM. Each method has its pros and cons, for
instance, qualitative methods often provide a false sense of certainty and are criticized for
their simplicity. Quantitative methods, on the other hand, are constrained by information
quality, time and resources (Wang et al., 2017). Hence, there is a need to build an integrated
DSS, comprising the benefits of both qualitative and quantitative tools.

We address the aforementioned research issues by proposing a DSS, which shall
accelerate the decision-making process in the domain of SCRM. The next section deals with
the review of proposed DSSs in the existing research along with various tools and techniques,
which are used for building the DSS for the present research.
2.2 Tools used for building the DSS
The usage of DSS in the domain of SCRM is relatively scanty. Kumar and Viswanadham
(2007) used a DSS adopting the case-based reasoning approach to evaluate the preventive and
interceptive actions against construction SC risk.
In the following section, we review the usage of various tools and techniques (in the
context of SC), which are used for the construction of the proposed DSS.
2.2.1 Systematic review:. We adopt a three-level-keyword based systematic review approach
to explore various (both proactive and reactive) RMEs and RMSs from a wide variety of
industries. The first level corresponds to keywords like supply chain risk OR supply chain
resilience OR supply chain vulnerability to capture the context of ‚ÄúSC Risk‚Äù. To capture the
context of ‚ÄúRisk Mitigation‚Äù, the second level keywords include mitigation OR management
OR response OR treatment OR protection. The final level corresponds to keywords like enabler
OR strategy OR strategies OR antecedents OR practice etc. All the levels are conjoined by
‚ÄúAND‚Äù set with each other and applied to the [Title, Abstract and Keyword] field only. We use
leading bibliographic databases like Web of Science, Scopus, Emerald, Taylor and Francis,
Springer, etc. The search results are further improved by imposing additional filters like
journal articles written in English and excluding conferences, book series, commercial
articles, etc.
The search results obtained from the review are used to explore various RMEs across
heterogenous case industries (summarized in Table 2). The table excludes some review
papers and articles which are based on the SC risk identification and assessment phase
(like Baryannis et al., 2018b; Tarei et al., 2018; Ho et al., 2015) and merely focuses on different
proactive and reactive RMEs (as per the definition in Section 1). Since SCRM as a domain
gained momentum after the year 2000 (Baryannis et al., 2018b), it can be observed that all the
articles appear in the range of the year 2000‚Äì2018.
RMSs (as per our proposed definition in Section 1) are alienated from those of
RMEs and classified as shown in Table 3. We have considered and summarized the
definitional significance of six RMSs, which appeared frequently in the prior literature.
2.2.2 Correspondence analysis. Correspondence analysis (CA) is a multivariate statistical
technique, used to establish the relationship between two sets of nominal variables
(RMEs and RMSs in this case) in the form of a perceptual map. The position of the variables
on the perceptual map is defined by the weighted chi-square distance between them.
Loureiro et al. (2015) applied CA to explore the relationship between the literature stream
of strategies and supply chain management. Moreno et al. (2016) used CA to establish the
correlation between the causes of hazards and consequences in biogas production. Oliva et al.
(2016) examined the relationship between agents and event for enterprise RM by CA.
Interested readers are suggested to refer to Greenacre (2017) for the detailed mathematical
steps of CA.
2.2.3 Fuzzy inference system. Decision-making related to SCRM always involves the
accumulation of tacit knowledge and expertise of key stakeholders. To automate the decision-
making cycle, tacit knowledge has to be encoded and transformed into a machine-
interpretable form. A popular method is a rule-based representation (with case-based being a
common substitute). The rules are not only easy to construct by simple if-then formalization
but also easily interpretable by both humans and machines (Baryannis et al., 2018a). The FIS
involves a process of choosing one or more (alternatives) solutions from a finite set of

3. Proposed DSS for SCRM
The ultimate objective of this study is to build a DSS containing all the methods discussed in
the previous section to assist the SC managers in the assessment of RMSs and the
implementation of necessary RMEs. The proposed DSS framework is explained in Figure 1.
We started exploring the RMEs from the systematic literature review by the three-level
search criteria mentioned in section 2.2.1 and listed the RMEs. Once the RMEs are listed
down, they are validated to ensure the significance of each RME on reducing the overall
impact of SC risk. An expert panel, consisting of SC practitioners from heterogeneous
industries and academicians are formed. The interview invitation was initially sent to
30 experts located across the country, based on the authors‚Äô personal and professional
contact by the convenience sampling procedure. After three weeks, email and telephonic
reminder requests were sent to the participants. Finally, 12 respondents agreed for the

‚Ä¢ To identify the root cause of (triggering events) of risk and

‚Ä¢ Examples include, redesign of SC, (modern) technology adaption,

interview. The response rate of 40% provides a good indication for a positive assessment of
the survey (Malhotra and Grover, 1998). Twelve experts are deemed an acceptable number
for a first-round but further surveys will be conducted in the future to ensure the validity. The
demographic background of each respondent, their professional experience, industry
categories and ownership structure of the industry are summarized in the appendices
(Table A1).
In the primary phase, the questionnaire was emailed to participants, where it was asked to
identify only significant RMEs and RMSs, given the choice of ‚ÄúYes‚Äù or ‚ÄúNo‚Äù for each item. The
primary survey aims to ensure the significance of each RMEs and RMSs and reduce
repetition and duplicate entries. For instance, RMEs such as aggregate demand and pool
demand; and RMSs such as risk-sharing, risk transfer and risk hedging are merged and
considered once. It resulted in a total of 62 RMEs (listed in Table 2) 6 RMSs (listed in Table 3).
The next stage pertains to the mapping of finalized RMEs to the corresponding RMSs. For
this, we have restricted the RMEs which are significant and relevant to the case company
(explained in section 4.1) and carried out the analysis of CA. The results obtained from CA are
used to categorize the RMEs according to various RMSs.
As shown in Figure 1, the final task is to assess (predict) an effective RMS and hence
implement the corresponding RMEs. The selection of an RMS primarily depends upon the
criticality of the risk score (both probability and severity of the risk event). We extend this by

10:
adding two variables, viz. ‚Äúresource availability‚Äù and ‚Äútime period of implementation.‚Äù The
resource availability and time period of implementation of RMEs of a firm dictate the choice
of RMS (whether to ignore or mitigate or share it) of a firm (Blackhurst et al., 2005). Since each
of the aforementioned variables is subjected to a high degree of subjectivity and hence cannot
be expressed precisely. We express each variable in form of linguistic categories, for instance
‚ÄúRisk score‚Äù has been categorized into two levels, acceptable and unacceptable according to
(their tolerance level) and their membership functions are defined accordingly. The linguistic
values and membership functions of other variables are summarized in Table 5. A snapshot
of modeling of the various decision variables in the fuzzy logic toolbox (MATLAB) is
provided in the appendices section (Table A3).
The next task is to map the linguistic values of the input variables to the output variables,
for which fuzzy inference system (FIS) is used. One of the crucial steps of FIS is the deduction
and formalization of if-then rules (Ghadimi et al., 2017). We conducted the second phase of
data gathering (interview) with the expert panel mentioned in Table A2. This time the
respondents were requested for face to face interview/discussion. Each interview lasted for
approximately 1 h with each respondent. During the interview, the expert was asked about
his choice of a particular linguistic value of RMS (say accept or mitigate) under each varying
combination of the input variables. The results obtained from the interview are summarized
in Table 6.
From Table 6, 12 if-then rules have been formulated with the levels mentioned above of
each variable. A sample rule is shown below for the explanation.
If the risk score is ‚ÄúAcceptable‚Äù and resource availability is ‚ÄúLow‚Äù and time period of
implementation (of the RMEs) is ‚ÄúLong-term‚Äù then prescribed RMS should be ‚ÄúControl‚Äù.
We have used Mamdani‚Äôs compositional rule of inference for building the FIS in the
current research. The four major steps of FIS are fuzzification, knowledge base (rules), fuzzy
inference mechanism and defuzzification (Oner and Oztaysi, 2017). The selection of the target
range for each input parameter was a part of the expert interview process. Triangular
membership functions with linguistic variable ‚Äúlow‚Äù, ‚Äúmedium‚Äù and ‚Äúhigh‚Äù have been chosen
for each input variable. The rules are combined by ‚Äúand‚Äù, centroid defuzzification method has
been utilized to simulate the FIS in the Fuzzy Logic Toolbox of MATLAB 2016A (Academic
License) in a 2.40 GHz processor speed, 8 GB RAM computer.
Considering the above settings of the FIS, we created an interface of a DSS based on the
MATLAB platform (of the same configuration), which considers the three input values and
prescribes the suitable RMS after processing the input information. The RMEs against each
RMSs are stored in local database. The RMEs list is displayed upon suitable selection of the
corresponding RMS. Further, the DSS provides the order of implementation of the RMEs
based on their distance from the cluster center, i.e. RMS. In other words, an RME which is
close to the RMS should be implemented first and so on. The snapshots and the detailed
workflow of the DSS are shown in Figures 2.
4. Applications of the proposed methodology
We demonstrated the efficacy of the proposed methodology considering the case of Indian
petroleum SC (IPSC). In the following section the description and importance of the case is
discussed.
4.1 Description and introduction about the case
With a daily demand of 4.1 million barrel, India is the third largest consumer of crude oil and
petroleum products globally accounting for 4.5% of world‚Äôs total oil consumption in 2015.
The oil and gas sector in India is among the six core industries of the country, and it
contributes about 15% to the overall GDP of the country. (Tarei et al., 2020). The country has

635 million metric tons (MMT) of proven oil reserves (at the beginning of 2015); with almost
half of the nation‚Äôs sedimentary territory is yet to be investigated, the oil and gas part gives
enormous opportunities for investment (US EIA, 2017).
Some of the significant risk drivers of (IPSC) are transportation (delivery system), quality of
the petroleum products, crude supply, customer‚Äôs order, legal/political regulation, economic
crisis and merger/ alliance (Tarei et al., 2018). The study mentioned above further reveals that
IPSC possesses an overall risk score of 34%. We extend the existing research by considering
the risk score as 34% (as the base one) and simulated two scenarios by changing the rest of
the variables (i.e. time period of implementation and resource availability). We evaluate the
RMS and the RMEs under these two scenarios in the context of IPSC and present the results.
As mentioned in the previous section (Section 3), we have focused on the case industry and
considered the RMEs which are relevant to IPSC for the analysis of CA. We adopted a
separate structured interview process focusing on the petroleum SC in the Indian context. A
panel of six experts was formed based on their professional expertise in the leading oil and
gas industries of the country. The profile of the experts is provided in appendices section
(Table A2). The average experience of each member is more than 15 years in the area of
sourcing (procurement), planning, refining and marketing (sales). After consulting with the
team, total 62 RMEs, which are explored from the literature review, are reduced to 28 due to
the uniqueness of Petroleum SC. (Some RMEs like dynamic assortment planning, multiple
customer accounts etc. are not relevant to the case industry and hence excluded). In the
subsequent step, we ask each expert to link the finalized RMEs to the corresponding RMS
based on their expertise (domain knowledge). For instance, if an expert feels that RME:
revenue management should be implemented, when the overall RMS is planned as accept,

4.2 Results of correspondence analysis
The output of the CA is expressed as perceptual maps. The positioning of RMEs on the two-
dimensional perceptual map represents the association or belongingness to the closest RMS.
Figures 3 represents the position of various RMEs and RMSs along the dimension 1 and
dimension 2 plane. The plane containing dimension 1 and 2 jointly explains 66% variance of
the total data. Dimension 1 explains the maximum variance (around 37%) of the data. The
figure reveals that ‚ÄúAccept‚Äù and ‚ÄúTransfer‚Äù are the two extreme RMS clusters along
dimension 1. Risk acceptance is preferred when the SC managers become resilient and ready
to face (challenge) the risk, and they intend to bounce back from negative consequence
incurred. On the contrary, risk transfer is strategized well beforehand of the occurrence of the
risk, which leaves little tasks to be done after the post math. Hence, dimension 1 can be
attributed to managers‚Äô ‚Äúpreparedness to face risk‚Äùto deal with risk.
Dimension 2 explains around 29% variability of the total data. As shown Figures 3 and
Figures 4 ‚ÄúAccept‚Äù and ‚ÄúAvoid‚Äù are shown as the two extremes of dimension 2. An
organization usually prefers risk acceptance strategy for minor risk events, especially when it
realizes that the cost of incurring a loss (due to risk) is less than the cost of preventing the risk.
On the contrary, risk avoidance is generally attributed to finding out the root-cause (potential
triggering events, which can turn into significant disaster) of risk and eliminating it by
changing the (physical) property of the SC. Hence, dimension 2 is regarded as ‚ÄúResource
Capability‚Äù of a firm to deal with risk.
We have also considered the third dimension 3, which contributes around 22% variance of
the total data. As shown in Figures 4 and Figures 5 ‚ÄúControl‚Äù and ‚ÄúAvoid‚Äù are two extreme
RMSs along dimension 3. ‚ÄúRisk Control‚Äù consists of regular day-to-day operational practices
such as, aggregate (pool) demand, weather forecasting, etc.; whereas ‚ÄúRisk avoidance‚Äù consists
of specific occasional RMEs such as Speculation, Operation rerouting, etc. Hence, dimension 3
can be attributed to the degree of ‚Äúsophistication‚Äù of RMPs.
Based on the results obtained from CA, and the perceptual maps (explained in the previous
sections), a hierarchal taxonomy is proposed (Figure 6). The segregation of RMPs is done,
based on the distance of each RMP from the nearest cluster center of RMS positioned on the
perceptual map. The taxonomy presents an unblemished representation of the RMSs with
their corresponding RMEs.
4.3 DSS application and demonstration:
In order to explain the working principle of the proposed DSS, we have formed and analyzed
the following two scenarios in the context of the IPSC.
4.3.1 Scenario 1. In the first scenario, we have considered various input parameters viz.
risk score, time period and allocated resources as 34%, 8 months and 7 units (out of 10),
respectively. The DSS recommends ‚ÄúRisk Mitigate‚Äù as the proper RMS under the given
conditions. It further provides the list of RMEs (shown in Figure 7) under the aforementioned
RMS. The RMEs are ranked according to their closeness from the cluster (RMS) center.
As shown in Figure 7, the results further indicate that supply incentives, flexible logistics and
strategic stocking are three topmost preferred RMEs under risk ‚Äúmitigation‚Äù strategy. To
understand the riskiness of the petroleum SC and to mitigate the negative consequence, the
Government of India has taken some policy initiatives recently. For instance, HELP
(Hydrocarbon Exploration Licensing Policy, 2016) was launched, which offers various

The ranking of various RMEs are shown in Figure 8. The result reveals that, multiple

Unlike the earlier profit-sharing mechanism (in NELP), the government does not need to
scrutinize the cost details of the individual players and hence the delays and disputes are
avoided effortlessly.
5. Discussion and implication
Irrespective of the case (industry) or the country, SCRM has been recently experiencing global
research attention from both practitioner and academician point of interest. The process of
SCRM starts from identification and prioritization of critical triggering (disruptive) events
and hence, their net (devastating) impact on SC and next, the design of suitable RMSs at
the top (strategic) level and implementation of RMEs at the real-world operational level. In
this regard, this research work contributes to the existing literature by establishing the
mapping (cohesion) between RMSs and RMEs. The relationships (between RMSs and RMEs)
are further used to construct a DSS. The DSS considers various critical parameters such
as risk score, the time period to implement the RMEs and resource availability as the input
variables and recommends the suitable RMS and corresponding RMEs and the order of their
implementation.
The contributions of this research work are summarized in the following section.
5.1 Theoretical implications
We have adapted various tools from heterogeneous disciplines, viz. CA, a multi-variate
statistical extensively used in the marketing and social science research and rule-based FIS to
capture the imprecise, vague and uncertain human judgmental information. The proposed
methodology is a hybridization of methodologies that does not seem to have been proposed in
the domain of SCRM.
We followed a systematic three level keyword search procedure to explore the various
literature on SC RMSs and hence the RMEs, irrespective of their nature of implementation
(proactive or reactive (resilient)), problem case industry, etc. Additionally, we have presented
and justified the definitional (theoretical) distinction between RMSs and RMEs, whereas the
existing (prior) literature has experienced the interchangeable usage of the aforementioned
terminologies.
The results of CA help us to obtain the underlying relationship between various RMSs.
Three underlying factors (dimension) viz. adaptive capability (courage) to deal with risk, short/
long term treatment of risk and sophistication of RMEs explain a significant proportion of
variation of the relationship between RMSs and RMEs.
We have conceptualized twelve rules considering various levels of three essential
parameters viz. risk score, the time period to implement RMEs and resource availability for RM
and to delineate appropriate RMSs. The rules are thereafter fed into a rule-based FIS. The FIS
acts as a knowledge base to make a decision, i.e. to choose an effective RMS. The FIS
additionally takes care of the impreciseness, vagueness and uncertainty of various input
parameters in terms of membership functions.

5.2 Managerial propositions
Based on the results obtained from the proposed research work, we have introduced
the following managerial propositions (MPs), which will provide critical insights to the
domain of SCRM and will assist SC practitioners to take the various short term and long-term
managerial decisions to deal with SC risk.
MP1. Both RMEs and RMSs hold separate (individual) definitional significance, and they
should be designed (or decided) at separate level (hierarchy) of an organization.
An RMS is decided by the top-level executives/managers (at the strategic level), and it shall
be aligned with the organization‚Äôs long-term (goals) mission and vision, competitor‚Äôs mindset
(action), etc. On the contrary, RMEs are the actual practices which are implemented at
the bottom (operational) level in order to nullify the negative consequence of risk events
(drivers). Once an RMS is decided (selected), the corresponding RMEs shall be designed and
assessed (evaluated) according to their preference (order/priority) of implementation. ‚ÄúRisk
management‚Äù in an organization is everyone‚Äôs responsibility. Successful implementation of
RM process is a collaborative approach (effort) ranging from people at the strategic level to
the operational level‚Äô.
MP2. Contextual variables of a firm/organization, like resource availability and time-
period of implementation (or planning horizon for implementing RMEs) play a
crucial role in SCRM process and hence selecting the proper RMS.
Most of the prior studies focus on the nature of the risk variables, like the likelihood, or severity
of risk and hence the overall risk score of the SC, while mentioning the RM cycle. The present
work, however, considers some additional variables like resource availability, time-period of
implementation (or planning horizon for implementing RMEs) which influence the decision-
making process while selecting the proper RMS and designing corresponding RMEs.
‚ÄúResource‚Äù of an industry is a broad term, which ranges from the (technical) knowledge of its
employees to financial capability (to hedge against the bad days). ‚ÄúTime period of
implementation‚Äù is the (tentative) planning horizon (short-term/long- term) which a firm takes
in order to develop the alternative RMEs, which is further dependent on several factors like
organization‚Äôs core competency, agility, etc. These variables are highly dependent on the
context or nature of the industry and they frequently change over time (dynamic), The results
of this research work shows that inclusion of the contextual variables improves the SCRM
process and hence the managers should not ignore such variables and must pay equal
attention beforehand,. In addition, the proposed DSS will help to avail (opt) different RMS at
different combinations of the aforementioned dynamic input variables.
MP3. The (proposed) DSS is expected to provide the organizations to gain a competitive
advantage by expediting the decision-making process in mitigating SC risk, with
comparatively more accuracy and at a lesser cost than the conventional methods.
Conventional decision-making is a process of selecting a rational choice from the available
alternatives, which is often prone to human (expert) bias, subjectivity, imprecise input data/
information, etc. Decision-making in the domain of SCRM strategy selection is not an
exception. In this regard, the proposed DSS encapsulates the tacit (unexpressed) expertise
knowledge in the form of rules and takes care of the imprecise (incomplete) information by the
fuzzy membership functions of the decision parameters. The DSS (after defuzzification)
produces crisp outputs, i.e. an RMS and ranks the corresponding RMEs. The proposed FIS
based DSS operates with the single click of a mouse and yields results with comparatively
less subjectivity bias (more accuracy). However, a wide range of experimental surveys need to
be conducted in the future to increase the generalizability and evaluate the benefits of
application of the proposed DSS.

6. Conclusion
SCRM (as a domain) has been experiencing incremental research attention in the recent
decades. While, most of the studies have focused on identification and prioritization of
triggering events of risk, literature regarding proposing various risk mitigation strategies
and practices (enablers) are relatively scanty. In order to address this issue, the present
research work has focused on developing a holistic model to predict the suitable RMS under
changing risk context and evaluate RMEs. In order to explain the efficacy of the proposed
research, we have additionally considered a case study of petroleum SC in the context of a
developing country (India). Established tools and techniques from heterogeneous discipline
such as marketing research (social sciences) and soft computing are concatenated together to
build a DSS. The DSS will facilitate the SC stakeholders and practitioners to make faster
decision-making (i.e. assessment of available RMSs) with more accuracy in an uncertain and
risky environment.
The prior literature has witnessed the interchangeable usage of RMSsand RMEs. We have
differentiated the terminologies in the context of SCRM without compromising with their
(terms) etymological definition. The results of our study further conclude that the
responsibility of ‚Äúrisk management‚Äù in an organization ranges from the strategic level to
operational level. The choice of an RMS (in strategic level) not only depends upon risk score
but also on some contextual variables like planning horizon and resource availability.
Thenceforth, the corresponding RMEs are implemented (in operational level).
Like any other research work, this research also is not free from limitations, which steer
the direction for future research opportunities. We have used the expertise knowledge of
various SC practitioners from the domestic (developing) country (India) to build the
conceptual framework. Hence, although the proposed framework is replicable, scope of
generalization of the results is limited. Future research can focus on cross country (both
developing and developed) analysis and analyses across industries (manufacturing, process
and service). The proposed DSS has the limitation of recommending the RM strategizes and
practices for familiar risk events only. The assessment and mitigation of sudden and
disruption risk, such as COVID-19 outbreak, are complicated and beyond the scope of the
present research. This study, however explicitly focuses on one step of SCRM process, i.e.
‚ÄúRisk Mitigation‚Äù. The SC managers and stakeholders shall not underestimate the dynamic
nature of risk. ‚ÄúRisk Monitoring‚Äù which is a subsequent step of ‚ÄúRisk Mitigation‚Äù, along with
consideration of dynamic nature of risk can be extended as future research work. Hence, the
future research work is paramount in the context of SCRM process as a whole.
